teamObjective.explanation: I am leading a new trick with no table lead. Playing the highest available non-trump single maximizes the chance I retain the lead and control tempo, which is important because an opponent is near finish.

partnerBelief.summary: Partner (player 2) has 3 cards remaining. No public plays have revealed partner's holdings, so I cannot infer specific cards; partner may be able to cover the trick if it is played low enough.

opponentBelief.summary: Opponent player 1 is near finish with only 2 cards remaining, and opponent player 3 has 3 cards. Their exact cards are unknown, but the near-finish player may hold a higher single or a trump that can beat a low lead.

actionRationale.primaryReason: I selected the 6 of clubs because it is the strongest single in my hand. Among four low non-trump clubs, leading the 6 gives my team the best chance to keep the lead against a near-finishing opponent; lower cards would be easier to beat.

whyNotAlternatives.reason: The 3 is the weakest card and would be very easy for an opponent to beat with almost any higher single or trump, likely ceding the lead to the near-finish opponent.
Only marginally stronger than the 3; it still does little to keep the lead and wastes a slightly higher card.
Stronger than the 3 and 4, but still lower than the 6 and therefore less likely to retain the lead if an opponent can play a higher card.

risks: All four of my cards are low non-trump singles, so any opponent with a higher card or a trump may beat my lead and take control.
The near-finish opponent may win the trick and use their remaining cards to finish the hand.
My partner may not be able to cover if the trick requires a card stronger than what partner holds.
riskAssessment.mitigation: By leading the 6 rather than a lower card, I make it more costly for opponents to take the lead. If the trick comes around, my partner may be able to cover, and I preserve my next-best cards for subsequent plays.
